items.title: Palo Alto PAN-OS VPN Flaw CVE-2026-0257 Actively Exploited in the Wild
items.desc: Active exploitation of PAN-OS GlobalProtect auth bypass since May 17. Limited VPN sessions established.
items.summary: Palo Alto Networks confirmed active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257 (CVSS 7.8), an authentication bypass in PAN-OS GlobalProtect portal and gateway components. Attackers can bypass security controls and initiate VPN connections. Limited attacks observed since May 17. CISA added it to the KEV catalog with a June 1 patch deadline. IoCs and hard-coded PoC client configuration values published.
items.importance: GlobalProtect is the VPN backbone for thousands of enterprises. An auth bypass with active exploitation means unpatched firewalls are one step from full network access. The CISA KEV listing confirms real-world threat.
items.noteworthy: Patch PAN-OS immediately. Search GlobalProtect logs for gateway-connected events matching PoC indicators (endpoint_os_version: 'Microsoft Windows 10 Pro 64-bit', empty source_user_info.domain). Block known IoC IPs. Enforce MFA on VPN.

items.title: Chinese Hackers Hijacked Google Workspace Rules to Steal Research Emails
items.desc: UNC6508 spent 2+ years inside North American research networks, using Google Workspace forwarding rules for exfiltration.
items.summary: Google's Threat Intelligence Group attributed a campaign to UNC6508 (China-linked) that compromised REDCap research servers across US and Canadian medical, academic, and military institutions since September 2023. The group deployed INFINITERED custom malware that trojanizes REDCap system files, harvests credentials, and persists through upgrades. They then rewired victims' Google Workspace email rules to auto-forward matching messages to attacker-controlled inboxes.
items.importance: Using the victim's own Google Workspace rules for exfiltration is a sophisticated technique that bypasses DLP and email monitoring. Over two years of undetected access to defense and medical research is a massive intelligence breach.
items.noteworthy: Audit Google Workspace forwarding rules for unauthorized entries. Check REDCap servers for INFINITERED indicators. Review external-facing research platforms for compromise. Implement conditional access policies for email rule changes.

items.title: M365 Copilot SearchLeak: One Click Exfiltrates Emails, Files, and MFA Codes
items.desc: Three chained bugs in Copilot Enterprise Search enable one-click data exfiltration via trusted microsoft.com links. CVE-2026-42824.
items.summary: Varonis Threat Labs disclosed SearchLeak, a one-click exfiltration attack against Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search (CVE-2026-42824, CVSS 6.5 Microsoft / 7.5 NVD). Three bugs chained: parameter-to-prompt injection in Copilot's q parameter, a race condition in response rendering that bypasses code-block sanitization, and CSP bypass via Bing's allowlisted domain. A single click on a real microsoft.com link could exfiltrate emails, calendar details, and MFA codes. Microsoft mitigated server-side.
items.importance: Copilot has access to every document, email, and MFA code in a tenant. A one-click attack via a trusted domain that bypasses all anti-phishing and URL filtering is devastating. This is the first serious Copilot-specific vulnerability chain.
items.noteworthy: Microsoft has patched this server-side — no customer action required. However, review Copilot Enterprise Search access policies. Consider restricting Copilot's data scope. Monitor for similar prompt injection patterns in AI tools.

items.title: LiteLLM 3-CVE Chain: Low-Privilege User to Full AI Gateway Takeover (CVSS 9.9)
items.desc: Default low-privilege account chains to full admin + code execution on LiteLLM proxy servers. Exposes all provider API keys.
items.summary: Obsidian Security disclosed a three-CVE chain in LiteLLM (AI gateway for 100+ model providers) rated CVSS 9.9. CVE-2026-47101 allows authorization bypass via allowed_routes wildcard, CVE-2026-47102 enables privilege escalation to proxy_admin via self-update, and CVE-2026-40217 is a sandbox escape in Custom Code Guardrails. A default internal_user can chain all three to take over the server, exposing every provider API key and all prompt/response data. Fixed in LiteLLM v1.83.14-stable (May 2).
items.importance: LiteLLM is the default open-source AI gateway. A full server takeover exposes every LLM provider key, all stored credentials, and every prompt/response pair. Any deployment still on pre-v1.83.14 is critically vulnerable.
items.noteworthy: Upgrade LiteLLM to v1.83.14-stable or later immediately. Rotate all provider API keys if the proxy was exposed. Restrict network access to LiteLLM admin endpoints. Audit logs for unauthorized key generation.

items.title: WordPress Plugin Supply Chain Attack: 1.2M Sites Targeted via PushEngage, OptinMonster
items.desc: Trusted JS files for three Awesome Motive plugins tampered to create admin backdoors when loaded by logged-in administrators.
items.summary: An attacker tampered with JavaScript files served by PushEngage, OptinMonster, and TrustPulse (all Awesome Motive properties, collectively reaching 1.2M+ sites). The poisoned script only activated when a logged-in WordPress admin loaded it — creating a rogue admin account and installing a hidden persistence plugin. OptinMonster and TrustPulse were exposed for ~25 minutes on June 12; PushEngage's window lasted hours. Only PushEngage has issued guidance; OptinMonster and TrustPulse have not responded.
items.importance: Three of the most popular WordPress plugins simultaneously compromised in a targeted supply chain attack. The admin-only trigger means automated scanners won't detect the backdoor. 1.2M sites potentially exposed.
items.noteworthy: If running any of these plugins, check admin user lists for unknown accounts. Audit installed plugins for hidden entries. PushEngage users should rotate CDN-served scripts. Consider switching from CDN-loaded to self-hosted plugin scripts.

items.title: NK Hackers Weaponize VS Code Auto-Run to Deliver Cross-Platform Malware
items.desc: Contagious Interview group uses VS Code 'runOn: folderOpen' to auto-execute malware when devs open cloned repos. 100 orgs targeted.
items.summary: Proofpoint attributed two campaigns (codenamed UNK_DeadDrop) to North Korea's Contagious Interview group. Over 250 emails targeted ~100 organizations across finance, crypto, education, and tech. The attack chain: phishing with GitHub repo links → victim clones and opens in VS Code/Cursor → 'runOn: folderOpen' triggers OS-specific malware loaders (Overlord Go framework) without user interaction. Targets span macOS, Linux, and Windows.
items.importance: VS Code auto-execution on folder open means opening a project is now a potential compromise vector. This is a supply chain attack on the developer workflow itself — no clicking, no running, just opening a folder in your IDE.
items.noteworthy: Audit VS Code/Cursor settings for suspicious 'runOn: folderOpen' entries. Disable automatic task execution in editor settings. Verify all cloned repositories before opening. Treat unsolicited GitHub repos as untrusted.

items.title: 152 Chrome Wallpaper Extensions: 105K Installs, Adware and Fake Traffic Network
items.desc: Network of 152 extensions across 38 publisher accounts harvesting IP, ISP, click data and routing traffic through ad fraud.
items.summary: Researchers discovered 152 Chrome new-tab wallpaper extensions across 38 publisher accounts and three backend domains (tabplugins.com, yowgames.com, chromewallpaper.com). 105,000 installs total. Extensions claim no data collection in store listings but privacy policies admit logging IPs, ISPs, click counts, and sharing with Google AdSense and DoubleClick. Extensions also manipulate new-tab pages for fake traffic monetization.
items.importance: 105K users running adware masquerading as wallpaper extensions. The deceptive privacy policy vs store listing disconnect is a systemic Chrome Web Store enforcement failure.
items.noteworthy: Audit installed Chrome extensions for wallpaper/new-tab add-ons from unknown publishers. Remove any matching the three backend domains. Use uBlock Origin. Chrome Web Store enforcement remains insufficient — extensions are still a primary attack vector.

items.title: Rust vs C/C++ Memory Safety CVEs: Why the Numbers Mislead
items.desc: Analysis shows Rust CVEs flagged for potential unsafety in unsafe blocks, while C/C++ CVEs reflect exploitable memory corruption. The comparison is misleading.
items.summary: A detailed analysis by Jakub Beránek demonstrates that comparing raw CVE counts between Rust and C/C++ is misleading. Rust CVEs related to memory safety are almost always in unsafe blocks and reported proactively as potential issues before exploitation. C/C++ CVEs typically reflect actual exploitable memory corruption. The post uses curl's libcurl as a case study — showing how a simple function call in C can cause memory unsafety without the developer realizing it, while Rust's ownership system catches similar issues at compile time.
items.importance: This is the definitive response to 'Rust has CVEs too' arguments. Rust memory safety CVEs are categorically different: they're pre-emptive reports about unsafe blocks, not post-exploitation disclosures of memory corruption. The distinction matters for language adoption decisions.
items.noteworthy: Use this analysis when evaluating language safety trade-offs. Rust's CVE count reflects a different — and more secure — disclosure culture. If your org is considering Rust adoption, this provides concrete evidence that Rust CVEs are less severe in practice.

items.title: Nvidia Raises $20B in Bonds for AI Infrastructure Push
items.desc: Nvidia's first debt offering in 5 years. $20B bond sale funds AI data center buildout. BTC miners pivoting to same infrastructure.
items.summary: Nvidia announced a $20 billion multi-part bond offering — its first debt issuance in five years — to finance AI infrastructure investments and refinance existing obligations. The offering underscores the relentless demand for AI compute. Simultaneously, Bitcoin miners are pivoting to AI and HPC services, with over $70 billion in contracts announced across the sector, leveraging their existing power infrastructure for GPU compute.
items.importance: Nvidia's $20B raise signals that the AI infrastructure buildout is accelerating, not slowing. The Bitcoin mining → AI pivot means GPU compute capacity is consolidating. This affects GPU availability, energy markets, and crypto mining economics.
items.noteworthy: Watch for GPU supply chain impacts. BTC miners pivoting to AI compute may reduce mining hash rate. The convergence of AI and crypto infrastructure creates new investment opportunities in HPC data centers.

items.title: US-Iran Peace Deal: Strait of Hormuz Reopening
items.desc: Trump announces complete US-Iran peace deal. Strait of Hormuz to reopen. Formal signing June 19 in Switzerland.
items.summary: President Trump announced that a peace deal between the US and Iran is 'complete,' ending months of conflict that began with joint US-Israeli strikes on Iran in February 2026. Pakistan mediated the agreement. The Strait of Hormuz is set to reopen upon signing, with a formal ceremony scheduled for June 19 in Switzerland. The framework deal marks the biggest breakthrough toward resolving the wider regional conflict that escalated after the initial strikes.
items.importance: The Hormuz reopening directly impacts global oil supply and shipping routes. Oil prices will react sharply. Crypto markets may see volatility as geopolitical risk premium unwinds. This is the most significant geopolitical development since the conflict began in February.
items.noteworthy: Expect oil price drops and shipping cost normalization. Monitor BTC correlation with oil — the Iran conflict premium has been supporting risk-off flows. Cyber threat landscape may shift — Iranian APT groups may pivot based on political directives.

items.title: Strategy Buys 1,587 BTC for $100M, Holdings Hit 846,842 BTC
items.desc: Saylor's Strategy resumes accumulation. Total holdings: 846,842 BTC worth ~$56B. ~4% of Bitcoin's total supply.
items.summary: Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) acquired 1,587 BTC for approximately $100 million between June 8-14, 2026, at an average price of ~$62,933 per BTC. Total holdings now stand at 846,842 BTC with a cumulative cost basis of $64.07 billion. The purchase comes just two weeks after Strategy's first BTC sale in four years, reaffirming its long-term accumulation strategy. Strategy now controls roughly 4% of Bitcoin's 21 million fixed supply.
items.importance: Strategy's purchase signals institutional confidence after a brief sell. At 846,842 BTC, they're the largest corporate holder by far. The resumption of buying after a brief dip into selling removes a bearish signal.
items.noteworthy: Watch for continued accumulation. Strategy's USD reserve also grew to $1.1B. The 4% supply concentration in a single entity is an ongoing systemic risk for Bitcoin's liquidity.

items.title: Kraken Launches CFTC-Regulated Perpetual Futures for US Traders
items.desc: First CFTC-regulated crypto perpetuals venue for US traders. Perps alongside spot, margin, and CME futures on Kraken Pro.
items.summary: Kraken launched CFTC-regulated perpetual futures for eligible US traders through its Bitnomial subsidiary. This is the first fully regulated crypto perps product available to US retail and institutional traders. Perpetuals now sit alongside spot, margin, and CME-listed futures on Kraken Pro. The launch brings a product onshore that US traders previously accessed through offshore exchanges.
items.importance: US traders finally have regulated access to the most popular crypto derivatives product. This legitimizes perps trading, brings it under CFTC oversight, and could shift significant volume from Binance/Bybit to regulated venues.
items.noteworthy: US-based traders can now access perps through Kraken Pro with regulatory protection. Monitor for volume migration from offshore exchanges. CFTC oversight means consumer protections that don't exist on offshore platforms.

items.title: Market: BTC $66.3K (+1.1%). ETH $1,792 (+4.2%). SOL $73.7 (+3.6%).
items.desc: Markets rally on US-Iran peace deal. ETH and SOL outperform BTC. Strategy buys 1,587 BTC.
items.summary: Crypto markets rallied on the US-Iran peace deal announcement. BTC at $66,302 (+1.07%), ETH at $1,792 (+4.22%), SOL at $73.74 (+3.60%). BTC market cap stands at $1.33T. ETH and SOL significantly outperforming BTC suggests risk-on rotation within crypto. The Hormuz reopening and oil price decline are removing geopolitical risk premium. Strategy's continued BTC accumulation provides a structural bid.
items.importance: Peace deal removes the biggest geopolitical overhang since February. ETH and SOL outperformance signals alt-season conditions emerging. BTC holding above $66K with declining geopolitical risk is constructive for a push toward $70K.
items.noteworthy: Watch $70K resistance — a break above confirms the bullish thesis. ETH at $1,792 is testing key resistance. The geopolitical de-escalation reduces tail risk for all risk assets. Strategy's $100M weekly buys provide structural support.

items.title: Chrome V8 Zero-Day CVE-2026-11645 Exploited in the Wild
items.desc: CVSS 8.8 out-of-bounds memory access in V8 actively exploited. Patch Chrome 149.0.7827.103 now.
items.summary: Google patched 74 Chrome vulnerabilities including CVE-2026-11645 (CVSS 8.8), an out-of-bounds read/write in V8 that allows arbitrary code execution inside the sandbox via a crafted HTML page. Active exploitation in the wild confirmed. This is the 5th Chrome zero-day exploited in 2026. Researcher '303f06e3' credited with discovery.
items.importance: Chrome is the most targeted browser. An actively exploited V8 zero-day means every unpatched Chrome user is a potential victim. The sandbox escape is the critical question — if paired with a sandbox escape, this is full system compromise from a single webpage visit.
items.noteworthy: Update Chrome to 149.0.7827.103+ immediately. Check Chromium-based browsers (Edge, Brave, Opera) for their own patches. Enable auto-updates. Consider using a separate browser profile for high-risk browsing.

items.title: Miasma Worm Hits 73 Microsoft GitHub Repos — Supply Chain Attack
items.desc: Self-replicating worm compromised 73 Microsoft repos across Azure, Microsoft, MicrosoftDocs, and Azure-Samples. GitHub disabled access.
items.summary: The Miasma self-replicating supply chain worm hit 73 Microsoft GitHub repositories across four organizations: Azure, Azure-Samples, Microsoft, and MicrosoftDocs. GitHub disabled access to affected repos. Miasma is a variant of the Mini Shai-Hulud worm publicly released in mid-May 2026. Microsoft has since restored some repos while keeping others offline as the investigation continues. The worm injects an information stealer into code.
items.importance: Microsoft's own GitHub repos being compromised by a self-replicating worm is a watershed moment for supply chain security. If Microsoft can't protect its own open-source infrastructure, the trust model for all GitHub-hosted software is called into question. The worm's self-replicating nature means it spreads autonomously.
items.noteworthy: Audit any dependencies pulled from Microsoft GitHub repos in the last 30 days. Pin dependency versions. Enable GitHub Dependabot alerts. Monitor for Miasma indicators of compromise. Consider mirroring critical dependencies to private registries.

items.title: IronWorm + Miasma Variant Hit npm: 50+ Packages Compromised
items.desc: Two supply chain worms hit npm simultaneously — IronWorm and a Miasma variant — distributing Rust-based infostealer across 50+ packages.
items.summary: JFrog reported multiple supply chain attacks hitting npm, with threat actors using malicious and poisoned versions of over 50 legitimate packages to distribute a Rust-based information stealer and a self-spreading worm. The Miasma worm variant targets npm packages, while IronWorm is a separate campaign. Both scrape developer secrets: environment variables, SSH keys, cloud tokens, and API credentials.
items.importance: npm is the largest package registry in the world. Two concurrent worm campaigns targeting it means the ecosystem is under active, automated attack. The self-replicating nature of these worms means they spread faster than manual takedowns can contain them.
items.noteworthy: Audit npm dependencies for any packages matching Miasma/IronWorm indicators. Pin all dependency versions. Consider using npm audit and Socket.dev for real-time supply chain monitoring. Rotate any credentials that may have been exposed.

items.title: One-Character Linux Kernel Flaw: Local Root + Container Escape (CVE-2026-23111)
items.desc: Use-after-free in nf_tables lets unprivileged users escalate to root and break out of containers. Working exploit published.
items.summary: Exodus Intelligence published a working exploit for CVE-2026-23111, a use-after-free in the Linux kernel's nf_tables packet-filtering code. A single-character bug lets an unprivileged local user escalate to root and break out of containers. The flaw was patched upstream on February 5, 2026, but many distros may not have deployed the fix. Working exploit code is now public.
items.importance: Local root + container escape from a single-character bug is the kind of vulnerability that powers privilege escalation chains. If you run multi-tenant containers or have untrusted users on Linux systems, this is a direct threat. The public exploit means script kiddies can weaponize it.
items.noteworthy: Verify your kernel is patched beyond February 5, 2026. Check distro kernel versions against the fix commit. For container hosts, ensure seccomp profiles and AppArmor/SELinux are enforced. The exploit requires local access — harden SSH and user authentication.

items.title: FROST Attack: Websites Track Your Apps via SSD Timing — No Permissions Needed
items.desc: Novel side-channel attack uses JavaScript + OPFS SSD timing to identify which websites and apps you use at 88.95% F1 accuracy.
items.summary: Researchers disclosed FROST, a novel side-channel attack that uses JavaScript and the Origin Private File System (OPFS) to time SSD operations. By measuring drive contention, a malicious website can determine which other sites and apps you have open — with 88.95% F1 accuracy. No native code, no extensions, and no permission prompts required. The attack works across browsers because it exploits the SSD's physical characteristics, not a browser bug.
items.importance: This is a fundamental privacy leak that no browser setting can fix. It exploits the physics of SSDs, not a software bug. Every modern browser supports OPFS, making this a cross-browser tracking vector that ad blockers and privacy extensions cannot mitigate.
items.noteworthy: No patch available — this is a hardware-level side channel. Mitigations include disabling OPFS in browser flags (may break web apps), using RAM-backed temp storage, or switching to a system without SSDs. For sensitive work, consider separate physical machines.

items.title: AI Agent Uncovers 21 Zero-Days in FFmpeg; Chrome Patches Record 429 Bugs
items.desc: Autonomous AI agent finds 21 zero-days in FFmpeg. Same week, Chrome ships record 429 patches in a single release.
items.summary: Two events within days: depthfirst's autonomous AI agent discovered 21 previously unknown vulnerabilities in FFmpeg, the media library used in almost everything that touches video. The same week, Google shipped Chrome 149 with patches for 429 security bugs — the most ever in a single Chrome release. The FFmpeg findings include bugs latent for 15-20 years, with a working RCE PoC developed. Chrome's 429 patches span V8, GPU, networking, and extensions.
items.importance: AI-powered vulnerability discovery is no longer theoretical — it's producing results at scale. 21 zero-days in a single library, found autonomously, at 10% of traditional cost. Chrome's 429-bug release signals that the vulnerability surface is expanding faster than manual review can keep up.
items.noteworthy: Patch FFmpeg to latest version. Sandbox all media processing. Update Chrome to 149.0.7827.103+. The AI vulnerability discovery trend means more CVEs are coming — prepare for increased patch velocity.

items.title: ChatGPT Lockdown Mode: OpenAI's Answer to Prompt Injection Data Theft
items.desc: OpenAI rolls out Lockdown Mode for ChatGPT personal accounts, restricting tools that could enable data exfiltration via prompt injection.
items.summary: OpenAI began rolling out Lockdown Mode to ChatGPT for eligible personal accounts. The feature restricts tools that could be used for data exfiltration via prompt injection attacks — limiting code interpreter, file uploads, and web browsing capabilities when sensitive data is present. OpenAI acknowledges Lockdown Mode does not prevent all prompt injection effects — a malicious instruction hidden in an uploaded file could still affect ChatGPT's behavior and cause incorrect answers.
items.importance: This is the first major platform-level response to prompt injection as a data exfiltration vector. For anyone handling sensitive data through AI assistants, Lockdown Mode is a necessary but insufficient mitigation. The admission that it doesn't fully prevent prompt injection effects is honest but concerning.
items.noteworthy: Enable Lockdown Mode in ChatGPT settings if handling sensitive data. Continue to avoid uploading sensitive documents to AI assistants. Treat AI-generated output as untrusted. This is a mitigation, not a solution — the underlying prompt injection problem remains unsolved.

items.title: Cisco Unified CM RCE (CVE-2026-20230): Exploit Code Goes Public
items.desc: Unauthenticated attacker can write files and escalate to root on Cisco Unified Communications Manager. PoC exploit code now public.
items.summary: Cisco patched CVE-2026-20230 in Unified Communications Manager, which lets an unauthenticated attacker on the network write files to the box and escalate to root. Proof-of-concept exploit code is already public. The vulnerability affects Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME. CISA has added it to the KEV catalog alongside Chrome and Arista flaws.
items.importance: Unified CM is the phone system backbone for thousands of enterprises. An unauthenticated file write + root escalation with public exploit code means every unpatched Unified CM is a ticking bomb. Voice infrastructure is often overlooked in patch management.
items.noteworthy: Patch Cisco Unified CM immediately. If patching isn't possible, restrict network access to Unified CM management interfaces. Audit for signs of compromise. CISA KEV listing means federal contractors have 14 days to patch.

items.title: CISA Adds Cisco, Chrome, and Arista Flaws to KEV Catalog
items.desc: Three new vulnerabilities added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog: Cisco Unified CM, Chrome V8, and Arista EOS.
items.summary: CISA added three vulnerabilities to its KEV catalog: CVE-2026-20230 (Cisco Unified CM RCE), CVE-2026-11645 (Chrome V8 zero-day), and an Arista EOS flaw. All are under active exploitation. The KEV listing requires federal agencies to patch within 14 days and serves as a priority signal for all organizations.
items.importance: CISA KEV is the de facto priority list for enterprise patch management. Three additions in one batch signals that attackers are actively targeting these vectors. The Arista EOS inclusion is notable — network infrastructure is increasingly in the crosshairs.
items.noteworthy: Check your environment for all three products. Prioritize patching in KEV order. The 14-day federal mandate is a good benchmark for all organizations.

items.title: Smart TVs Turned Into Web-Scraping Proxies for AI via Free Apps
items.desc: Researcher reverse-engineers Bright Data's iOS SDK embedded in free apps, turning smart TVs into residential proxies for AI web scraping.
items.summary: A researcher reverse-engineered the iOS SDK that Bright Data embeds in consumer apps, documenting how it turns devices — including smart TVs — into residential proxies for web scraping. Free apps on smart TVs quietly route traffic through the device, using the home's IP address to scrape websites for AI training data. The device owner is unaware their bandwidth and IP are being used as a proxy node.
items.importance: Your smart TV is now a proxy server for AI companies. This is the next evolution of the 'free app monetization' model — instead of ads, your device's IP address is the product. For home lab operators, this means your home IP could be flagged for scraping activity you didn't initiate.
items.noteworthy: Audit smart TV apps. Remove any free apps that don't need network access. Monitor your home IP for unusual outbound traffic patterns. Consider using a VPN at the router level to prevent IP-based tracking. Block unknown outbound connections from IoT VLAN.

items.title: HTTP/2 Bomb: Remote DoS on NGINX, Apache, IIS, Envoy & Cloudflare
items.desc: New HTTP/2 vulnerability enables remote denial-of-service across major web servers and reverse proxies.
items.summary: Researchers discovered a remote denial-of-service exploit affecting multiple HTTP/2 implementations including NGINX, Apache HTTP Server, IIS, Envoy, and Cloudflare. The 'HTTP/2 Bomb' exploits how different servers handle HTTP/2 stream multiplexing, causing resource exhaustion with minimal bandwidth. The attack can take down servers with a single connection.
items.importance: HTTP/2 is the backbone of modern web traffic. A DoS that works across every major web server and CDN is a systemic vulnerability. For anyone running web infrastructure, this is a must-patch across the entire stack.
items.noteworthy: Update NGINX, Apache, IIS, and Envoy to latest versions. Check Cloudflare status for their mitigation. Implement rate limiting and connection limits as defense-in-depth. Monitor for unusual HTTP/2 connection patterns.

items.title: Unpatched Windows Search URI Leaks NTLMv2 Hashes
items.desc: Windows Search URI handler can be abused to leak NTLMv2 hashes to remote attackers. No patch available.
items.summary: Researchers disclosed an unpatched issue in Windows Search URI handling that can be exploited to disclose a user's NTLMv2 hashes. An attacker can trigger the leak by getting a user to click a specially crafted link, which causes Windows to authenticate to a remote SMB share, sending the user's NTLMv2 hash. The hash can then be cracked offline or used in relay attacks. No patch is currently available from Microsoft.
items.importance: NTLMv2 hash theft is a classic attack vector that keeps resurfacing. The hash can be cracked offline or relayed to authenticate as the user. With no patch available, the only mitigation is blocking outbound SMB traffic and educating users about suspicious links.
items.noteworthy: Block outbound SMB (port 445) at the firewall. Disable NTLM authentication where possible via Group Policy. Educate users not to click suspicious links. Monitor for unexpected SMB outbound connections. This is unpatched — assume all Windows versions are vulnerable.
